item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

Event details

item.title: Squak Mountain Celebration Hike
item.description:
Wednesday, March 25, 2026 at 12 PM

Location: Squak Mtn- Bullitt Fireplace Trailhead, Mountainside Dr SW, Issaquah, WA

Join IATC Vice President Selena Eon for an afternoon hike on Squak Mountain to celebrate all of the hard work volunteer crews have put in to restore these trails in the wake of the 2024 bomb cyclone. Throughout 2025, over 130 IATC volunteers worked to remove debris and restore tread to trails on Squak over the course of 25 work parties. We’ll meet at Mountainside Drive Trailhead \(also known as Bullitt Fireplace Trailhead\) and work our way up to Old Griz, where a substantial portion of the 2025 trail work took place. We’ll also view the landslide sites on Phil’s Creek before heading down the Eastside Trail back to the trailhead. Trails: Mountainside Drive Trail, Old Griz, Phil’s Creek, Eastside Trail Meeting Location: Mountainside Drive Trailhead Date & Time: Wednesday, March 25th, 12:00 PM - 5:00 PM Duration: 5 hours Distance: 5 miles Elevation Gain: 1300 feet NOTE: This hike includes 1300 feet of elevation gain over 5 miles, making it a moderate to difficult hike.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

Event details

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Thursday, March 26, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Jazz Month Tour
item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 7:30 AM

Location: The Royal Room, 5000 Rainier Ave South, Seattle, WA

March’s Women in Jazz Month comes alive as award-winning singer-songwriter Eugenie Jones lights up the Royal Room with her national Women in Jazz Month Tour. With stops in Denver, San Francisco, Atlanta, and Seattle, this tour joins a worldwide tribute to the women who shaped jazz, honoring trailblazers such as Billie Holiday, Nina Simone, and Ella Fitzgerald. At the Royal Room, Jones will blend beloved songs from female jazz legends, her own Jazz Week \#4 ranked originals, imaginative spins on vintage jazz, and timeless rhythm and blues classics. She’ll be joined by the remarkable talents of pianist Peter Adams, bassist Osama Afifi, and drummer Maria Wulff. As a Seattle Jazz Hall of Fame inductee, the first vocal recording artist to receive Earshot Jazz’s Album of the Year award, a two-time Vocalist of the Year winner, and a Jazz Journalists Association’s Jazz Hero Award winner, Jones has received high praise for her vocal prowess. Paris Move magazine describes Jones as “One of the most beautiful voices in the United States; a consummate interpreter who slips effortlessly into the soul of her songs.” Preview Jones’ music, watch videos, or learn more about this gifted entertainer at https://ffm.to/eugeniejonesmusic. Get your tickets today for this unforgettable night of exquisite vocals, masterful musicianship, and stirring storytelling, all in tribute to the enduring legacy and vibrant contributions of women in jazz. Tickets: https://www.strangertickets.com/events/180924339/eugenie-jones

Friday, March 27, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Friday, March 27, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Saturday, March 28, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: BiggerPockets Value-Add Conference
item.description:
Saturday, March 28, 2026 at 8 AM

Location: Seattle, Wa, 98121, Seattle, WA

Real estate investors looking to sharpen their execution and scale for smarter deals will not want to miss the **first-ever BiggerPockets Value-Add Conference** in Seattle on March 28, 2026. This one-day, tight-knit event has only 120 seats available and is designed specifically for experienced investors actively executing deals. Led by seasoned local investor and Million Dollar Zombie Flips host James Dainard, alongside BiggerPockets experts Dave Meyer and Henry Washington, the conference offers a rare opportunity to learn how top investors scale deals in today’s competitive real estate market through stronger underwriting, strategic renovations, optimized financing, and disciplined execution systems. Investment to attend the one-day conference starts at $500, with a limited number of spots available HERE.  The in-person event features powerful speakers, delivering  practical, action-based strategies investors can implement immediately, including: Team building and legal structure for sustainable growth Underwriting and deal structuring across asset types Capital stack and financing strategies (hard money, DSCR, BRRRR) Construction and project management systems that protect margins Renovations and design decisions that directly impact ROI Scaling operations with stronger vendor management and streamlined processes This experience is designed for investors with three or more completed deals who are actively executing BRRRRs, flips, or small-scale development projects and looking to build repeatable, scalable systems. With Seattle’s competitive housing market and continued investor activity, the conference offers timely insight into how local operators are navigating tighter margins, rising costs, and evolving financing strategies. In addition to the conference, on March 29, a limited Premium Property Tour will take attendees inside active Seattle investment properties, where James and the speakers will walk through real deals and answer questions in small groups.

Location Link: Broadview Branch Room Location: Meeting Room Registration Required: Registration is not required. Detailed Description: This service is free and available to individuals only. The free service is not available for business tax returns. To receive tax help, bring the following documents:, Required: Photo identification, Social Security cards or Individual Taxpayer Identification Numbers \(ITIN\) for everyone listed on the tax return., Proof of income, for example, W2s and 1099s \(Social Security benefits\), Official documents on Social Security Administration letterhead, Medicare card \(if Social Security number is followed by an "A"\), Be prepared to provide information about health care coverage for each person on the return., Recommended: Last year&'s tax return, if available, Bank account and routing numbers for direct deposit of refunds, Photocopies or computer print-outs of any of these documents willbe accepted., Due to federal budget cuts the IRS is unable to provide the Library with certain tax forms and instruction booklets., Most IRS forms are available online at www.irs.gov ., You may make free black-and-white prints of the forms from Library computers: www.spl.org/Print , Thanks to our partner AARP . item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Saturday, March 28, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.

Event details

Meet the exhibit curators and local historians who contributed to the exhibit. And be the first to view our newly updated lower-level exhibits while you're here! Enjoy prohibition era-inspired mocktails, snacks, and more!

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Saturday, March 28, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

Event details

item.title: PCC Cooking Class - Savory Sichuan
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Columbia City PCC, 3610 S Edmunds St, Seattle, WA

Learn to make traditional Chinese dishes at home! In this cooking class, you’ll pick up techniques and tips for capturing the flavors of Sichuan cuisine. Get practice infusing oil with chilis and hand-grinding spices for a sauce as you create deeply flavorful dan dan noodles. You’ll also try your hand at methodically rolling and flattening dough with scallions for savory pancakes, which you’ll pair with a scratch-made dipping sauce. By the end of class, you’ll be ready to recreate the entire menu in your kitchen.  Contains pork and dairy.  About the instructor: Our seasoned instructors and chefs have been chosen to be a part of PCC Cooks because of their culinary expertise and sincere enthusiasm and talent for teaching.  This is a hands-on format class for adults.  This cooking class is available at multiple PCC locations and dates. item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

Featuring the work of Katie Metz, Elizabeth Gahan, Perri Lynch Howard and more. Join us and our featured artists for an opening reception on Thursday, March 5, 5:30-7:30 pm.

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

Event details

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

Event details

item.title: Kameelah Janan Rasheed: we leak, we exceed
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Kameelah Janan Rasheed \(b. 1985, East Palo Alto, CA; lives and works in Brooklyn, NY\) is a self-described “learner” who creates multi-media works that explore the poetics-pleasures-politics of Black knowledge production, information technologies, \[un\]learning, and belief formation. She often integrates found images and words alongside her own writing to create collaged wall installations and experimental video works. Across her work, Rasheed investigates the revision and life cycles of text, embracing expanded and embodied forms of writing and reading. we leak, we exceed will activate the unique volume and multiple vantage points of the Henry’s double-height gallery, drawing together threads from physics, Black critical thought, and information theory to create an immersive environment that interrogates the spatial and social implications of compression. A common process used in data storage, spatial organization, and information systems, compression abbreviates and collapses complex ideas into more simplified forms. Rasheed questions the way compression comes at the cost of nuance and creates unrecoverable losses. She draws parallels between the compression of information and the containment of people, both physically and through the structuring and defining of identities. Through a network of video, sound, and architectural mark-making, Rasheed proposes alternatively what she calls “an embrace of Black excess and expansion” as a liberatory practice.

item.title: Natasha Zimmers with Naja Lund Aparico - 'Tiny Hiker' and 'Seasons by the Lake: Adventures in Greenl
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Third Place Books Lake Forest Park, 17171 Bothell Way NE,, Lake Forest Park, WA

Welcome spring by celebrating the joy of exploring the great outdoors! Third Place Books welcomes authors Natasha Zimmers and Naja Lund Aparico to our Lake Forest Park store for a storytime featuring Natasha's book Tiny Hikers and Naja's book Seasons by the Lake: Adventures in Greenland. This event is free and open to the public. RSVP is not required. Readers of all ages welcome! About Tiny Hikers . . . "Offers a realistic, joyful depiction of a young adventurer and reflects the author's experience as an outdoor educator. A standout debut that will inspire young readers to explore the outdoors." -- School Library Journal \(SLJ\), starred review The tiny hiker finishes loading up her pack and excitedly gets on the trail, leading her family through the ancient trees toward their beach campsite. At first it's fun to explore--she finds a hollow log, wriggles under a fallen tree, and spots a slimy slug But then the trail starts getting steeper, and when her legs get tired, she trips on a root and takes a hard fall. Now her knee hurts, her elbows hurt...everything hurts She just wants to give up. But after a little break, a tasty snack and an exciting glimpse of the beach, the tiny hiker feels better and decides she will make it. With one final scamper over some giant beach logs, she finds the beach. Time to play And at the end of the long day, after sitting by the campfire, the tiny hiker gets cozy in her sleeping bag and falls fast asleep. About Seasons by the Lake. . . Spend a year experiencing the seasons in Greenland with two Inuit siblings. Brothers Minik and Nuka have a whole year of adventures ahead in Greenland, including finding ice shards from glaciers in the spring to picking crowberries during the summer when the sun barely sets. The darkness of winter reveals Arsarnerit, or the Aurora Borealis, above as the two whisper about the Inuit myth behind the lights. Greenlandic Inuk author Naja Lund Aparico combines a sweet sibling story with an exploration of the seasons in the Arctic featuring stunning illustrations by German and Choctaw illustrator Alex Nees that captures the sweeping landscape. Natasha Zimmers loves being outside in all seasons and weather, but her favourite place to be is on a wild west coast beach, backpacking with her own not-so-tiny hikers. She teaches outdoor education and loves seeing the world through the eyes of her students as they discover the wonders of nature. Naja Lund Aparico is a Greenlandic Inuk author with a background in international relations and currently a second year MFA graduate student at the Institute of American Indian Arts \(IAIA\) with a focus on creative non-fiction. Having grown up in Greenland, she now lives in the Pacific Northwest with her family. Aparico’s children’s books and essays are centered on the indigenous identity with an emphasis on kin, heritage, and the environment. Her US debut picture book, SEASONS BY THE LAKE, will be published by Dial / PRH in March of 2026, while her second picture book \(untitled\) is set to be published by Candlewick in late Spring of 2027, and her third picture book will be published by Dial/PRH in late 2027. Want a signed edition of the featured book, but can't make it to the event? Order through our website or over the phone, and write your request for a signature or personalization in the comments field at checkout. Please call the hosting store if you're placing your order within 24 hours of the event. For media or access inquiries, please email events@thirdplacebooks.com or call our Lake Forest Park store at \(206\) 366-3311.

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Sunday, March 29, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Monday, March 30, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

Enjoy all-day TV coverage of college basketball alongside cutting-edge golf and multisport simulators, a locally-inspired menu, and custom beverage packages. item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Monday, March 30, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: EMBODIED ALTARS DANCE CLASS
item.description:
Monday, March 30, 2026 at 6:30 PM

Location: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center, 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA

EMBODIED ALTARS is an all-levels dance class hosted by Daybreak Star Radio. Designed by Indigenous Chicana artist and El Sueño director, Alicia Mullikin. Dancers will utilize Indigenous principles within movement, meditation, and song as a way to practice gratitude, honor the land, and recognize their ancestors. The class is for those who are interested in reflection, intention setting, and a discovery of their own stories. No dance experience necessary. Class Time: Mondays, 6:30-8PM No class 9/15/25 No class 10/13/25 Venue: Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center 5011 Bernie Whitebear Way, Seattle, WA 98199 Sliding Scale: $10-$20\* per class. Sign Up Now!   This venue is ADA accessible w/ ADA bathrooms and a wheelchair lift to the second floor. Class will be held in the ballroom space, this area has 5 ADA parking spaces. There are an additional 25 parking spots in our lot, there is ample parking space along the road leading up to Daybreak star as well as the Discovery Park North Parking lot. What to bring: water, comfortable clothes to dance in, journal, your personal identity, a desire to explore new pathways and an open mind/heart. Hosted at Daybreak Star Indian Cultural Center Produced by United Indians of All Tribes Foundation In Partnership with El Sueno Dance & Daybreak Star Radio “Indigenize the Airwaves” “Art. Culture. Reclamation.” \*Talk to us about BIPOC and Queer/LGBTQIA2S\+ Scholorships\* Please contact @el.sueno.dance on IG for private conversations about this topic.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Tuesday, March 31, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: March Madness Watch Party at Five Iron Golf - Seattle Capitol Hill
item.description:
Tuesday, March 31, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Five Iron Golf - Seattle Capitol Hill, 1525 11th Ave Suite 100, Seattle, WA

Five Iron Golf is your College Basketball HQ this March! Gather your crew and book 1–2 simulator bays for up to 12 people \(6 people per bay\) for a bracket-busting watch party experience. Enjoy all-day TV coverage of college basketball alongside cutting-edge golf and multisport simulators, a locally-inspired menu, and custom beverage packages. Book your watch party for March 19 or 20 and you'll automatically be entered to win a 20-person happy hour with drinks and appetizers — a $1,000 value! Group packages can include reserved event space, team-specific décor, a dedicated staff member, full-screen and/or picture-in-picture viewing, a customizable menu, beverage packages, free club rentals, multisport simulators, and table games. Select the "Small Event" option at booking to ensure simulator bays are placed side-by-side. Availability of select features varies by location. Promotions: Happy Hour – "It's 5i o'Clock Somewhere" \(Monday–Friday, 3–6 PM\) Enjoy $5 bottomless boneless wings, $10 bottomless bone-in wings, $5 select beers/cans/cocktails, $5 dips \(guacamole, hot honey ricotta, hummus\), half-off pizzas, and $5 featured shots. Dine-in only at participating locations; simulator rental may be required. 5i After Dark – "Swing In, For Less"  \(Sunday–Thursday 9 PM to close | Friday–Saturday 10 PM to close\) Features $29/hr simulator rentals plus the same great food and drink specials as Happy Hour, with wings priced per dozen rather than bottomless. Dine-in only at participating locations; simulator rental may be required.

Event details

item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Tuesday, March 31, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

item.title: Power Connect: Bombo
item.description:
Tuesday, March 31, 2026 at 4:30 PM

Location: Bombo Italian Kitchen, 1600 9th Ave, Seattle, WA

Description Power Connect at Bombo Italian Date: Tuesday, March 31, 2026 Time: 4:30 PM Location: Bombo Italian Presented by: U.S. Bank Tickets: $25 for members and $35 for guests Join GSBA for a new Power Connect at Bombo Italian on Tuesday, March 31 at 4:30 PM. Power Connect is our signature networking series-an easygoing space to meet fellow LGBTQ\+ and allied professionals, reconnect with familiar faces, and build relationships that support your business and our community. Come as you are, bring a colleague, and expect the standard Power Connect vibes: warm welcomes, real conversation, and connections that keep going after the event. What to Expect A welcoming, relationship-first environment Space for casual conversation and meaningful introductions A great chance to meet GSBA members, partners, and friends An easy way to plug into the GSBA community Accessibility If you have access needs or accommodation requests, please reach out to GSBA in advance and we'll do our best to support you: membership@thegsb.org Thank You to Our Presenting Sponsor Power Connect: Bombo is presented by U.S. Bank, supporting LGBTQ\+ and allied businesses and helping strengthen connections across our region.

item.title: Make a difference for cats and dogs in need!
item.description:
Wednesday, April 1, 2026 at 12 AM

Location: Pawsitive Alliance, 14150 NE 20th St. F1-233, Bellevue, WA

Are you interested in using your time, talent, and energy to support the vision of a healthy and happy home for every cat and dog in Washington? If yes, then please consider joining our Board! Pawsitive Alliance, a volunteer driven tax-exempt nonprofit organization, is having an open call for Board Members. Founded in 2005, the mission of Pawsitive Alliance is to help end the euthanasia of adoptable dogs and cats in Washington by increasing adoptions, supporting spay and neuter programs, and improving pet retention. Pawsitive Alliance advances are mission through our three programs: -  Adoption program: \#WhyNotMePets campaign, highlighting long-term homeless pets, and supporting adoption events to help homeless finds homes -  Spay/Neuter: providing vouchers and financial and logistical support to increase surgeries and access to end pet overpopulation and suffering -  Pet retention: collecting and distributing pet food and supplies and offering pet ID tags and microchips to keep pets thriving with their caretakers Our programs and activities prioritize increasing services for pets, people, and places across Washington state with few resources. Pawsitive Alliance’s biggest strengths are marketing, organizing, fundraising, and collaborating.  We work with more than 50 human and animal-welfare partners each year. Since 2005, we have helped over 8,000 animals find homes, delivered over 170,000 pounds of donated pet food, and facilitated more than 11,000 spay and neuter surgeries. The Pawsitive Alliance Board is a hands-on group of committed individuals, investing their time and resources to support our mission.  As we celebrate our 20th anniversary in 2025, and look to the future, we are looking to add individuals from diverse backgrounds to join the Board and help the organization’s continued success and growth.  Ideal candidates are committed to our mission, can commit at least 10-15 per month to the organization, are able to represent the organization in the community, are ready to engage in strategy setting, fundraising and networking, and bring a well-rounded set of skills to a unique and hard-working team.​ Pawsitive Alliance is always interested in bringing greater diversity and new areas of expertise, energy, and skills to the Board. For more information on the organization, refer to our website at www.pawsitivealliance.org.  Please fill out our Board Application if you share our passion for helping to end pet homelessness in Washington and are interested in serving and helping end pet overpopulation. Those interested in applying should email our Board Recruitment Committee at info@pawsitivealliance.org.

item.title: Beyond Mysticism: The Modern Northwest
item.description:
Wednesday, April 1, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

image: Drift No. 2, 1936, Malcolm M. Roberts, American, 1913-1990, tempera on board, 25 1/2 x 20 5/8 in., Seattle Art Museum, Eugene Fuller Memorial Collection, 37.103, © Malcolm M. Roberts, photo: Scott Leen. Inspired by the region’s natural beauty and rapidly changing city, this exhibition showcases artists who told the raw story of Seattle in the 20th century. By sharing the Pacific Northwest’s perspective through trending art styles—including Social Realism, Surrealism, and Abstract Expressionism—these artists offered a unique take on Modernist art and challenged the “mystic” label ascribed to their works. Spearheading the movement were local icons Mark Tobey, Kenneth Callahan, Guy Anderson, and Morris Graves—but the range and breadth of Pacific Northwest art goes beyond the “Big Four” of the Northwest School. Featuring more than 150 pieces, this exhibition showcases works that investigate the complexities of daily life in the region. Whether applying an ecocritical lens or producing dreamy and uncanny visuals, these artists expressed the duality of the natural world and city life to warn of the environmental and social impacts of industrialization. From Malcolm Roberts to Kenjiro Nomura, learn how Northwest artists applied their regional perspective to broader artistic developments to create a new form of American Modernism. The exhibition is accompanied by a fully illustrated catalogue authored by Theresa Papanikolas, SAM’s Ann M. Barwick Curator of American Art.

Dine-in only at participating locations; simulator rental may be required. 5i After Dark – "Swing In, For Less"  \(Sunday–Thursday 9 PM to close | Friday–Saturday 10 PM to close\) Features $29/hr simulator rentals plus the same great food and drink specials as Happy Hour, with wings priced per dozen rather than bottomless. item.title: TADAIMA: I'm Home
item.description:
Wednesday, April 1, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Museum of History and Industry, 860 Terry Ave N, Seattle, Seattle, WA

After more than 80 years, a treasured collection returns “home” to Seattle’s Japanese American community. In 1942 dozens of traditional Boys’ and Girls’ Day dolls were entrusted to Bailey Gatzert Elementary School by Japanese American families facing forced incarceration. Most were never reclaimed after the war. Artist Miya Sukune uses recent research in MOHAI’s collection, as well as interviews with Japanese American survivors and their descendants, to create an installation called “Tadaima,” meaning “I’m home.” TADAIMA: “I’m Home!” Bringing Seattle’s Girls’ and Boys’ Day Dolls Home reconnects the dolls with their traditional role of protecting and blessing children. It invites visitors to celebrate the resilience, memory, and enduring traditions of the Japanese American Community.

Event details

item.title: Samantha Yun Wall: What We Leave Behind
item.description:
Wednesday, April 1, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Samantha Yun Wall’s new paintings use overlapping silhouettes of female figures as portals to unknown spaces and different temporal realms. Impetus for the new body of work is a Korean folk tale in which the Pasque flower is symbolic of a grandmother who passed away without the loving care of her grandchildren. It is a story of melancholy, loss, and remembrance. The delicate hair on the flower’s stem differentiates it, and the artist gives the plant a surreal aspect in some of her paintings, replacing the flower’s center with a watchful eye.

item.title: Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light
item.description:
Wednesday, April 1, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Seattle Art Museum, 1300 First Avenue, Seattle, WA

Anila Quayyum Agha: Geometry of Light invites you to become part of the art. Agha, a Pakistani American artist, animates spaces with her large-scale sculptural installations. Suspended from the ceiling, Agha’s steel cubes are laser-cut with intricate designs that project geometric shadows onto the visitor. She draws on both the light and dark of her own life, using South Asian art practices to convey the gender discrimination she faced growing up as a young girl in Pakistan. An in-depth exploration of Islamic architecture, art, and identity, this is the first solo exhibition of a Pakistani American artist in SAM’s 90-year history. Agha is carving a name out for herself in the art world—visit the Seattle Asian Art Museum for an immersive and illuminating experience.

item.title: Boren Banner Series: Camille Trautman
item.description:
Wednesday, April 1, 2026 at 11 AM

Location: Frye Art Museum, 704 Terry Ave, Seattle, WA

Every photograph is a reminder that the act of framing is never neutral. In Camille Trautman’s first solo museum exhibition in their hometown, the Seattle-born Duwamish artist uses photography and video to challenge colonial narratives and counter Indigenous erasure. The exhibition presents selections from their ongoing series The North American LCD—spectral self-portraits staged in varied natural landscapes. Rooted in Trautman’s process of coming out as transgender, the images show the artist’s body partially obscured by large LCD screens. The series examines how both landscape photography and digital media can shape or distort cultural identity, often by promoting or denying visibility.

item.title: The Maine
item.description:
Wednesday, April 1, 2026 at 5:30 PM

Location: Roseland Theater, Seattle, WA

Seven albums in, and The Maine's new release You Are Ok is arguably the band's most considerable sonic leap in their twelve-year career. What makes The Maine, The Maine is their ability and inspiration to take risks, all while staying tuned in and consistently exceeding the expectations of their fans. 2019 is shaping up to be another milestone year for these desert dwellers, which kicked off with their second ever headlining music festival in their hometown. You Are Ok marks The Maine's first top 10 album in its premiere on the charts \(peaking at \#8\), and also came in at \#1 independent record, \#2 rock album, \#2 vinyl album, and \#3 alternative album. Known for their live show, The Maine is taking You Are Ok around the globe this year including stops on their home continent, across South America, Europe, and more. The band will spend their summer on the Sad Summer Festival, co-headlining the inaugural year of a touring music festival in the US. The Maine is John O'Callaghan, Pat Kirch, Jared Monaco, Garrett Nickelsen, and Kennedy Brock.

item.title: Figure/Ground: New Criteria
item.description:
Thursday, April 2, 2026 at 10 AM

Location: Henry Art Gallery, 15th Ave NE & 41st St, Seattle, WA

Figure/Ground: New Criteria is inspired by cultural historian Sarah Lewis’s concept of “groundwork aesthetics,” exploring figure-ground relationships and their connection to social tension. It builds on Lewis’s work, which—set against the expansion of Stand Your Ground laws across the United States since 2005, laws that broadly define rights to use lethal self-defense against perceived threats—describes a range of artistic interventions that reimagine “the literal and figurative meaning of ground.” Primarily featuring works from the Henry collection created in the twenty-first century, Figure/Ground reflects a period in which hard-won civil rights and claims to self-determination have been eroded across the US, disproportionately affecting Black, Brown, LGBTQ\+, and other marginalized communities.
